data.title: European Markets Head for Higher Open
data.description:
European markets were poised for a higher open on Friday, extending a rally that has driven regional benchmarks to record highs.

Those moves came as OpenAI raised $6.6 billion in a share sale that valued the company at $500 billion, reinforcing bullish sentiment on artificial intelligence and related technologies.

Investors also looked past the ongoing US government shutdown, which has so far shown limited economic impact.

Attention now turns to fresh Eurozone unemployment data and Swiss inflation figures.

In premarket trade, Euro Stoxx 50 futures rose 0.2% while Stoxx 600 futures gained 0.3%.

data.title: Saksiam Leasing's Asset Quality Looks Resilient — Market Talk
data.description: Saksiam Leasing's asset quality looks resilient, Thanachart Securities' Rawisara Suwanumphai says in a research report as the brokerage maintains the stock's buy rating. The Thai company's disciplined loan growth strategy has preserved its credit health through the title loan nonperforming loan cycle in 2022-2024, the analyst notes. Given over 95% of its funding is linked to floating rates, the financial services provider benefits from rate cuts. However, the brokerage cuts its 2025-2027 net profit forecasts for the company by 2%-5% to partly reflect slower loan growth assumptions. It lowers the stock's target price to THB4.60 from THB5.60. Shares are 1.6% lower at THB3.76. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Food group Princes Group eyes London IPO
data.description:
UK's food and beverage firm Princes Group said on Friday it is considering an initial public offering to list on the London Stock Exchange.

The company exports its products to more than 60 countries and has more than 8,000 customers globally, it said in a statement.

data.title: J D Wetherspoon reports higher annual profit on resilient demand
data.description:
British pub group J D Wetherspoon reported a more than 10% rise in annual adjusted pretax profit on Friday, helped by resilient demand for its drinks and pub grub.

Before separately disclosed items, the company reported an adjusted pre-tax profit of 81.4 million pounds ($109.33 million) for the year ended July 27, compared with 73.9 million pounds last year.

data.title: Expansion of post-smolt facility Tytlandsvik Aqua
data.description:
Grieg Seafood is pleased to announce that Tytlandsvik Aqua is initiating its planned expansion with the development of Halls 5 and 6 at their post-smolt facility, starting building work in March 2026.  The aim is to have the first smolt installations in Hall 5 by August 2027 and in Hall 6 by September 2028.

AKVA group will continue as equipment supplier to ensure standardized technology at the facility.

Tytlandsvik Aqua’s development plan has from the start included six post-smolt halls with a production capacity of 1,500 tons per year. Infrastructure has been prepared for these six halls. It is the last two, halls 5 and 6, that will now be expanded.

– Tytlandsvik has long worked with a goal of completing the facility with 6 halls, the increase in capacity is important for the further work with post-smolt in the region. We are looking forward to continuing the good collaboration with Grieg Seafood and Bremnes Seashore, says Nils Viga, General Manager, Tytlandsvik Aqua.

– We see that the work done with post-smolt over many years is yielding good results. Our experience shows that when large post-smolt is released into the sea, mortality is halved. We also see that it significantly improves lice control. In recent years, around 60% of the company's fish have not needed lice treatment at all, due to the use of large post-smolt together with other preventive measures. We welcome the planned expansion of Tytlandsvik Aqua, says Nina Willumsen Grieg, CEO, Grieg Seafood.

About Tytlandsvik Aqua

Tytlandsvik Aqua AS is owned by Bremnes Seashore AS, Grieg Seafood Rogaland AS and Vesthavbruk AS in three equal parts. The plant is located in Hjelmeland municipality in Ryfylke. TytlandsvikAqua AS is under construction and has the capacity to produce 6,000 tons of "large smolt" for delivery to fish farmers, mainly in Rogaland.

About Grieg Seafood

Grieg Seafood ASA is one of the world's leading salmon farmers. Following completion of the Transaction announced 17 July 2025, our operations will have a more geographically focused footprint in southwestern Norway with farms located in Rogaland and headquarters in Bergen. Grieg Seafood ASA was listed at the Oslo Stock Exchange in June 2007.

Sustainable farming practices are the foundation of Grieg Seafood's operations. The lowest possible environmental impact and the best possible fish welfare is both an ethical responsibility and drive economic profitability.

To learn more, please visit www.griegseafood.com.

data.title: REG - Wellnex Life Limited - Issue of Shares
data.description:
RNS Number : 9820B Wellnex Life Limited 03 October 2025  

AIM Announcement                                                                                            3 October 2025

Wellnex Life Limited (ASX/AIM: WNX)

Issue of Shares

Wellnex Life Limited ("Wellnex" or the "Company") (ASX/AIM: WNX), announces the issue of 2,183,948 new ordinary shares of no par value in the Company ("Ordinary Shares") at a reference price of A$0.22 (£0.11) per share, in lieu of cash fees due for capital markets transaction services, including the dual listing on AIM, and investor relations purposes. 1,953,948 of the Ordinary Shares being issued are being issued to Reach Markets Pty Ltd or its nominees.

Admission and Total Voting Rights

Application has been made for admission of the 2,183,948 Ordinary Shares to trading on AIM. It is expected that admission will become effective and dealings in the Ordinary Shares will commence on AIM at 8.00 a.m. on or around 8 October 2025.

The new Ordinary Shares are being issued fully paid and ranking pari passu in all respects with the Company's existing Ordinary Shares.

Following Admission, the Company's issued share capital will comprise of 69,955,476 Ordinary Shares, none of which are held in treasury. Accordingly, the figure of 69,955,476 may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the share capital of the Company under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules and the provisions of the Company's constitution.

This AIM announcement has been authorised by the Board of Wellnex Life Limited (ASX/AIM:WNX).

data.title: All currencies will be stablecoins by 2030: Tether co-founder
data.description:
Tether co-founder Reeve Collins expects “all currency” to become stablecoins by 2030 as part of a broader shift that will see all forms of finance go onchain. 

“All currency will be a stablecoin. So even fiat currency will be a stablecoin. It’ll just be called dollars, euros, or yen,” said Collins in a wide-ranging interview during Token2049 in Singapore. 

“A stablecoin simply is a dollar, euro, yen, or, you know, a traditional currency running on a blockchain rail by 2030,” he added. 

Collins argues that stablecoins will be the primary method for transferring money within the next five years, as the benefits of tokenized assets have become too compelling for traditional finance to ignore.

“Probably before that, because you’re still going to use dollars. But it depends on what your definition of stablecoin is. The definition of stablecoin is essentially that you’re moving money on a blockchain,” he added. 

US crypto shift was the best thing to happen

Collins said that the best thing to ever happen to the crypto market was the positive “shift in stance” toward the sector by the US government this year.

He argued that many large TradFi firms were too afraid to enter the industry out of fear of government scrutiny, and while there is still some gray area surrounding the industry, it’s a very different ball game these days.

The Tether co-founder stated that this shift has opened the “floodgates,” as the traditional finance world is scrambling to enter the crypto sector, with blockchain-based stablecoins being a key focus due to their inherent utility.

“Every large institution, every bank, everyone wants to create their own stablecoin, because it’s lucrative and it’s just a better way to transact. And so those floodgates are open, and what it’s going to lead to is that soon, there won’t be CeFi and DeFi,” he said.

“There’ll be applications that do things, move money, give loans, do investments, and it will be a mix of the kind of the old, traditional style investments, and then the DeFi types of investments.”

The tokenization narrative is strong

Collins said tokenized assets offer far greater transparency and efficiency than non-tokenized assets — given that they can be moved quickly across the globe without middlemen — which in turn offers more potential upside.

“That is why the tokenization narrative is so big, because everyone realizes the increase in the utility that you get from a tokenized asset versus a non-tokenized asset is so significant that even the same two assets, just once they’re moved onchain, since the utility increases, that means the return increases.” 

Downsides of going fully onchain

However, Collins acknowledged there were also risks to such a monumental shift in global finance, such as the security of blockchain bridges, smart contracts and crypto wallets.

Crypto hacks and social engineering are also key issues that need to be addressed, he said, though he emphasized that overall levels of security are “improving.”

“And so the old trade off is still going to remain there… which is if you want to be fully in control … you can do that, but it’s technically complex,” said Collins.

“If you want to trust a third party like you do traditionally with banks, there are a lot of those services like the custodial versus non-custodial, so that those services will get more robust, and people will have more options moving forward. So yes, there are always risks in technology,” he concluded.

data.title: Russia Services PMI Falls to Near 3-Year Low
data.description:
The S&P Global Russia Services PMI fell to 47.0 in September 2025, down from August’s three-month high of 50.0.

This marked the third contraction in the services sector over the past four months and the steepest decline since December 2022, driven by a sharp drop in new orders.

Despite the sustained downturn in new sales, Russian service providers continued to expand their workforce.

Job creation was solid overall and the fastest since January 2024.

On the pricing front, input cost inflation accelerated to a five-month high, fueled by rising supplier, wage, and utility costs.

However, output (selling) price inflation eased amid strong market competition and efforts to stimulate demand.

Looking ahead, business sentiment improved, though it remained below the historical trend, as firms expressed cautious optimism regarding greater economic stability and a potential increase in customer numbers.

data.title: UK's Beauty Tech Group prices IPO at 271 pence a share
data.description:
UK's Beauty Tech Group (IPO-BEAU.L) on Friday priced its initial public offering price at 271 pence per share, implying a market capitalisation of 300 million pounds ($402.90 million).

($1 = 0.7446 pounds)

data.title: Beauty Tech Unconditional Dealings to Start Oct 8
Beauty Tech Conditional Dealings to Start on London Stock Exchange Friday
Asahi Says Unclear When Production, Shipping Will Resume After Cyberattack
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

Japan's top beer maker remains hamstrung by a cyberattack that has ensnarled its local production and shipping operations.

"We are still unable to receive orders or ship products," an Asahi Group spokesperson said on Friday, four days after it said that it had suspended order and shipment operations at group companies in Japan.

"Although the production systems at our factories have not been affected by the cyberattack, production has been suspended at many Asahi Group factories in Japan due to the inability to ship goods," the spokesperson said.

Asahi has approximately 30 factories in Japan for alcoholic beverages, soft drinks, and food products.

There's no clear outlook for when production and shipping will resume, said the brewer, which makes Japan's most popular beer, Asahi Super Dry.

As an emergency measure, it accepted orders manually for a limited selection of products--including Super Dry--on Wednesday, with deliveries scheduled for Friday or later.

It was still manually accepting limited orders for soft drinks and food products as of Friday. For alcoholic beverages, it is prioritizing the shipment of orders received on Wednesday, and hasn't accepted any new orders.

The company is reviewing future plans for accepting alcohol orders, the spokesperson said.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com
